subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear and focused title/abstract indicating front- and rear-wing aerodynamics.
Research question is explicitly stated and revisited throughout.
Appropriate mixed-method approach outlined with literature review, CFD and wind-tunnel data.
weaknesses: Minor digressions into general aerodynamics reduce tight focus.
Key methodological details (mesh size, boundary conditions, validation) are omitted.
Variable lists and workflow diagrams are incomplete, hindering full methodological clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student communicates the aerodynamic topic with sufficient clarity (band 3-4), formulates a clear research question that remains connected to the discussion (band 5 but slight breadth), and presents a generally complete methodology though missing critical details (band 3). These combine to an overall mid-band performance, meriting 4/6.

strengths: Solid application of Bernoulli, boundary-layer and vortex concepts to F1 examples.
Sample drag calculation demonstrates competent use of formulae.
Terminology such as downforce, Reynolds number, CFD is mostly accurate and consistent.
weaknesses: Occasional factual errors (using water density instead of air).
Symbol and notation inconsistencies (Câ vs C_d, miswritten formulas).
Overreliance on popular-science sources; insufficient peer-reviewed references.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work shows good subject knowledge with relevant use of fluid dynamics concepts and mostly correct terminology (band 3-4). However, misstatements of physical properties, symbol errors and weak scholarly foundations prevent it reaching the top band. A score of 4/6 reflects solid but imperfect mastery.

strengths: Argument is logically structured around theory, optimization and conclusion.
Analysis of different wing angles and track configurations remains relevant to the question.
Claims are supported by examples and figures, building a coherent thread.
weaknesses: Critical evaluation of sources and methods is superficial.
Quantitative depth is limited; uncertainty and error analysis are largely absent.
Counter-arguments and methodological limitations are not fully explored.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Analysis is clear and relevant (band 4-6), argument development is well structured (band 7-9), but critical evaluation is superficial (band 1-3). The blend of these performances yields an overall mid-range critical thinking score of 5/12.

strengths: Follows EE conventions: title page, word count, TOC, numbered headings and bibliography.
Figures, tables and equations are integrated and legible.
Layout generally supports navigation and readability.
weaknesses: Page numbering is inconsistent and out of sequence.
Some captions spill onto following pages, interrupting flow.
Table of contents lacks accurate pagination for subsections.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation meets core structural and formatting requirements (band 3-4) but exhibits layout inconsistencies that impair readability (band 1-2 for visual elements). Overall performance in presentation sits in the lower half of the top band, warranting 3/4.

strandSelections.reasoning: Technical termsâdownforce, trailing-edge separation, Y250 vortex, CFD, Reynolds numberâare mostly used correctly and consistently. Some French-English hybrid phrasing ("force d'appui ou portance nÃ©gative"; "vitesse de la fluide"), symbol mix-ups (Câ for C_d) and a few mistranslations show that terminology control is good but not fully precise; thus the strand fits comfortably in the 3â4 band, lower half.

The essay is organised into contextual theory, flow dynamics, design optimisation and conclusion, creating a logical argumentative thread. Within sections, claims are usually supported by cited examples or figures, and the student revisits the balance between downforce and drag repeatedly, building a coherent argument. Nevertheless, occasional descriptive passages and limited counter-argument mean the sophistication remains moderate rather than exemplary.

The student outlines an intended mixed-method approach (literature review, CFD, wind-tunnel data) and provides equations for downforce, drag and Reynolds number. Nonetheless, there is little evidence that original data were generated or that cited CFD images are the studentâs own work. Source selection is mostly popular-science websites, LinkedIn articles and journal papers, giving only partial evidence of informed choice. Important procedural details (mesh size, boundary conditions, validation steps) are missing, so the methodology is present but not complete or fully appropriate.

The essay demonstrates solid understanding of fundamental fluid-dynamics conceptsâBernoulli, angle of attack, boundary-layer separation, vortex generationâand applies them to Formula 1 examples such as the Mercedes W11 and Red Bull RB15. Sample calculations (drag at 300 km hâ»Â¹) show competent application, though numerical work is sparse and sometimes contains symbol errors. Misstatements like referring to air density as that of water and occasional oversimplifications prevent the work from reaching excellent mastery.

Critical appraisal of sources, methods or limitations is sparse. Apart from acknowledging that Bernoulli theory is idealised and noting possible trade-offs of laminar flow, the student rarely questions validity, data reliability or alternative explanations. Evaluation of the CFD images is brief and largely assertive. The work therefore shows only superficial evaluation, aligning with the 1â3 band (mid-band selected).

The title, abstract details (word count, session) and introduction clearly indicate that the essay will examine how front- and rear-wing design affects speed and handling in Formula 1. Despite occasional linguistic inaccuracies, the central aerodynamic focus is explicit from the outset, and headings/sub-headings keep that focus visible throughout the paper. Minor digressions and some repetition prevent it from reaching the highest level, but overall the topic is communicated with satisfactory clarity and purpose.

Images, tables and equations are integrated and generally legible, though resolution varies and some captions spill onto subsequent pages. The lay-out supports reading but spacing and numbering of pages jump (1,3,5â¦). These minor inconsistencies warrant the lower half of the 3â4 band.

The research question is stated verbatim on page 1 and is specific: it asks how wing design influences both speed and cornering performance. Subsequent sections (2.1â2.3) and the conclusion return to this dual outcome, demonstrating an ongoing connection. However, the question is broad enough to encompass multiple variables (angle, endplates, vortex generation) and the essay sometimes drifts into general aerodynamics; this slight loss of tight focus merits the lower half of the top band rather than a 6.

The student analyses three CFD rear-wing angles, compares drag/lift implications, and contrasts aerodynamic set-ups for Monaco versus high-speed circuits. These demonstrate relevance to the question, but the treatment remains largely qualitative; quantitative evaluation of results or uncertainty is limited. Consequently the analysis is clear and generally relevant but lacks the depth and consistent evidence expected in higher bands.

The essay follows EE structural conventions: title page with word count, table of contents, numbered headings and sub-headings, bibliography. Figures are labelled and referenced. All required elements appear complete, so the higher band is justified.

annotations.comment: In the drag equation you list water density (1.293 kgÂ·mâ»Â³) instead of air density (~1.225 kgÂ·mâ»Â³). Use correct fluid properties for accurate calculations.
Word count of 3985 is within the 4000-word limit, satisfying formatting requirements.
The title clearly states the investigation focus on front and rear wing aerodynamics, demonstrating effective topic communication and alignment with EE conventions.
The research question is specific and repeated verbatim, but it spans several variables and sometimes the essay drifts into broader aerodynamics. Consider tightening focus to key wing parameters.
Page numbering appears out of sequence in the footer, which can confuse readers. Ensure all pages are consecutively numbered.
The introduction clearly sets context but ends abruptly in listing independent and controlled variables without completing the enumeration. Provide a full variable list.
Figure 1 is described but not critically analyzed. Explain how its pressure distribution specifically informs your argument about wing design.
The footnote relies on a general tutoring website (Alloprof) rather than primary scientific literature. Replace with peer-reviewed sources to strengthen subject knowledge.
Coefficient notation uses âCââ rather than the conventional âC_dâ for drag. Standardize terminology to avoid confusion.
The methodology overview mentions CFD and wind-tunnel data but lacks critical details (mesh size, boundary conditions, validation procedure). Include these to demonstrate completeness.
Section headings in your theoretical framework raise questions rather than providing analysis. Reframe these as clear objectives or hypotheses to enhance focus.
The term âBernouilleâ is misspelled and the explanation lacks depth beyond the idealized principle. Correct the spelling and discuss its real-world limitations.
Several figures cite LinkedIn and YouTube rather than academic sources. Balance popular-media with peer-reviewed articles to strengthen scholarly rigor.
The text on Figure 8 describes pressure but does not link back to research question. Clarify how these pressure maps inform wing design performance.
The methodology section lists multidisciplinary concepts but lacks a flowchart or timeline. Include a detailed plan or diagram to illustrate research steps.
The downforce formula is miswritten as \(F_p=\tfrac12C_p\rho A\tfrac{v^2}{2}\). Correct to \(F=\tfrac12C_L\rho A v^2\) and define each symbol clearly.
Section 2.2 overview explains laminar and turbulent regimes but offers no quantitative thresholds or examples. Include numerical comparisons to reinforce analysis.
Figures 9â11 present CFD results without quantitative lift or drag values. Add tables or annotations to compare performance metrics directly.
Reynolds number formula is correct, but units and example calculation are missing. Show a real-case calculation for a typical wing section at 300 kmÂ·hâ»Â¹.
In the 300 kmÂ·hâ»Â¹ drag example you state ~4466 N but omit the km/hâm/s conversion. Detail each step to ensure methodological clarity.
Paragraph interpreting CFD configurations is clear but lacks uncertainty analysis (e.g., mesh sensitivity or error bounds). Discuss reliability and limitations.
The conclusion effectively summarizes key points but reiterates many introduction statements. Instead, synthesize implications and propose further research directions.
The bibliography shows diverse sources but formatting is inconsistent (capitalization, use of italics, DOI inclusion). Adopt a single citation style rigorously.
Table of contents omits page numbers for several subsections, reducing navigability. Include accurate pagination for all entries.
Some technical terms are mixed French-English (e.g., âforce d'appuiâ vs âdownforceâ). Choose one language and maintain consistency throughout.
Engagement with personal planning and reflection is absent. Consider adding a section evaluating your research process, challenges, and learning.
Discussion rarely counters its own claims. Introduce counterarguments (e.g., limitations of high downforce on tire wear) to strengthen critical analysis.
Critical evaluation of CFD and experimental methods is limited. Reflect on potential biases of simulations and wind-tunnel scaling errors.
Lack of peer-reviewed journal articles for core aerodynamic theory weakens scholarly foundation. Integrate studies from leading fluid dynamics journals.
The section on endplates describes function but could be enriched by showing CFD flow-field comparisons with and without plates.
Several URLs in the bibliography lack access dates or are broken across lines. Check each link for functionality and format consistently.
Layout inconsistency: some captions spill onto the next page, interrupting flow. Adjust spacing so figures and captions stay together.
Consider adding a brief section evaluating how regulatory constraints (e.g., FIA rules) influenced design choices to deepen contextual understanding.
Sentence structure and grammar errors (âL'avancement du technologiesâ) hinder clarity. Perform a detailed proofreading or peer review for language accuracy.
The student defines the variable âpâ twice for drag and downforce using water density. This redundancy suggests confusion; unify definitions and use correct air density.
The paper lacks a dedicated limitations section. Explicitly discuss methodological and data constraints to underscore critical evaluation.
